title: The Kalam Cosmological Argument
tagline: Whatever begins to exist has a cause — including the universe
p1: Whatever begins to exist has a cause.
p2: The universe began to exist.
p3: Therefore the universe has a cause — outside space, time, and matter.
evidence: The Big Bang confirmed the universe had a beginning, and the 2003 Borde-Guth-Vilenkin theorem shows that any on-average expanding universe cannot be past-eternal. A cause of all space, time, matter, and energy must itself be spaceless, timeless, immaterial, and enormously powerful — and, William Lane Craig argues, plausibly personal, since only a free choice explains how a timeless cause produces an effect with a beginning.
practice_q: Without looking: state both premises of the Kalam exactly — what is the careful wording of premise 1, and why does 'Who made God?' miss it?
convo: Have you ever wondered what caused the universe to begin — do you think it could have started from nothing at all?

title: The Leibniz Contingency Argument
tagline: Why is there something rather than nothing?
p1: Everything around us is contingent — it exists, but it might not have.
p2: Contingent things require an explanation outside themselves.
p3: The chain of explanations must terminate in a necessary being whose non-existence is impossible — and that is what we mean by God.
evidence: Leibniz asked why there is something rather than nothing. Even an eternal universe would still be contingent — age doesn't make a thing explain itself, and a pile of contingent things is still contingent. So the explanation must be a necessary being that exists by its own nature. Alexander Pruss defends the Principle of Sufficient Reason behind this: science itself assumes reality has explanations.
practice_q: Without looking: explain the difference between a contingent and a necessary being — and why even an eternal universe would still need an explanation.
convo: Here's a question I love: why do you think there is something rather than nothing at all?

title: The Thomistic Cosmological Argument
tagline: Right now, something must be holding everything in existence — Aquinas's unmoved mover
p1: Everything that moves or changes is moved by something else.
p2: A chain of movers cannot go back infinitely in a simultaneously operating series.
p3: There must be a first unmoved mover — Pure Act — requiring nothing outside itself.
evidence: Aquinas is not describing a chain back through time, like grandfather to father to son, but simultaneous metaphysical dependence right now: your existence this moment depends on physical laws, which depend on the constants of nature, and this present-moment chain must terminate in something that just is, with no further dependence. Offered as a pointer, not a knockdown proof, it sketches what classical theists mean by God.
practice_q: Without looking: why is Aquinas's chain of movers not like grandfather–father–son — what kind of dependence is he actually talking about?
convo: What do you think is holding everything in existence right now, at this very moment?

title: The Fine-Tuning Argument
tagline: The universe's constants fall within extraordinarily narrow life-permitting ranges
p1: The universe's fundamental constants sit within extraordinarily narrow life-permitting ranges — change them and no stars, planets, or life exist.
p2: This precision is not well explained by chance or necessity.
p3: The best explanation is intentional design by an intelligent Creator — an inferred, honestly contested conclusion.
evidence: The cosmological constant is fine-tuned to roughly 1 part in 10^120 — measured, peer-reviewed physics, and the data is conceded even by atheist physicists such as Roger Penrose and Fred Hoyle. Physicist Freeman Dyson: 'The universe in some sense must have known we were coming.' The debate is not whether the tuning is real but what it means; design is the inferred — and honestly contested — conclusion.
practice_q: Without looking: what exactly do atheist physicists concede about fine-tuning, and what remains the contested inference we draw from it?
convo: Did you know the universe's basic settings sit on a knife-edge for life to be possible? What do you make of that?

title: The Moral Argument
tagline: Objective moral duties need a ground beyond human opinion
p1: Objective moral values and duties — things genuinely right or wrong regardless of opinion — exist.
p2: Objective moral duties need a grounding beyond human opinion, and without God there is none.
p3: Therefore God exists as the foundation of objective moral truth.
evidence: The Holocaust was evil — not just unfashionable. This argument never claims atheists can't be good; many are kind and self-sacrificing. The question is quieter: what makes good actually good, for all of us? Human opinion changes, yet cruelty's wrongness doesn't change with the vote. And the Euthyphro dilemma has a third answer: God neither invents goodness by decree nor answers to a higher standard — his own nature is the standard.
practice_q: Without looking: what is the difference between 'atheists can't be moral' (which this argument never says) and the grounding question it actually asks?
convo: Do you think some things — like cruelty to a child — are wrong no matter what anyone believes? Where do you think that standard comes from?

title: The Ontological Argument
tagline: The very concept of the greatest conceivable being presses toward his existence
p1: God is defined as the greatest conceivable being — nothing greater can be imagined.
p2: A being that exists in reality is greater than one that exists only in the imagination.
p3: So if God existed only in the mind, we could conceive of something greater — a contradiction; the greatest conceivable being must exist in reality.
evidence: Debated for a thousand years since Anselm of Canterbury, this feels like a trick at first — yet Alvin Plantinga's modal version, using possible worlds, is rigorous enough to have convinced many professional philosophers. Gaunilo's 'perfect island' objection fails because an island has no intrinsic maximum, while maximal greatness includes necessary existence. Best held as a searching pointer to God's necessary existence, not a proof that settles everything.
practice_q: Without looking: reconstruct Anselm's reasoning, then answer Gaunilo — why can't the same logic prove a perfect island exists?
convo: Here's a puzzle philosophers have argued about for a thousand years: could 'the greatest conceivable being' really exist only in our imagination?

title: The Argument from Consciousness
tagline: The existence of mind cannot be explained by matter alone
p1: You have inner experiences — the feeling of warmth, the colour red, the taste of coffee.
p2: No purely physical account explains why physical processes are accompanied by inner experience.
p3: A universe created by a conscious God is exactly what you'd expect to produce conscious minds.
evidence: David Chalmers calls this the 'hard problem of consciousness': even a complete neuroscientific account of the brain leaves unexplained why there is something it feels like to taste coffee or see red. You could know every fact about wavelengths, cells, and neurons and still not know what red looks like until you saw it. Richard Swinburne argues consciousness is the single most powerful piece of evidence for theism.
practice_q: Without looking: what is the 'hard problem of consciousness', and why doesn't a complete map of the brain solve it?
convo: Science can map what your brain does when you taste coffee — but why do you think it feels like anything at all from the inside?

title: The Argument from Reason
tagline: If our minds are just evolved survival machines, why trust the reasoning that says so?
p1: If naturalism is true, our beliefs are produced by blind physical processes selected for survival, not truth.
p2: Survival and truth are different things — a false belief can help you survive just as well as a true one.
p3: So on naturalism we have no reason to trust the very reasoning that led us to naturalism — while our trust in reason points to a rational Creator of rational minds.
evidence: C.S. Lewis pressed this self-refutation of naturalism, and Alvin Plantinga sharpened it into the evolutionary argument against naturalism. Darwin himself confessed 'the horrid doubt' about whether convictions of a mind developed from lower animals are trustworthy, and atheist neuroscientist Patricia Churchland concedes that evolution selects for survival, not for true belief. The Christian has an answer: our minds are reliable because a rational God made them in his image.
practice_q: Without looking: why can't you rescue naturalism by testing your reasoning empirically — what makes the problem self-referential?
convo: If our brains were shaped only to help us survive, why do you think we can trust them to discover truth?

title: The Argument from Beauty
tagline: The existence of profound beauty points beyond the physical world
p1: The experience: music that brings tears, a sunset that stops you cold — beauty that consistently feels like it means something beyond itself.
p2: The options: on atheism it is only pleasant neural firing with no objective referent; yet it stubbornly feels objectively real and pointing.
p3: The best explanation: profound, transcendent beauty is better explained by a beautiful Creator than by blind naturalism.
evidence: C.S. Lewis called the longing beauty produces 'Joy' — literature, nature, and music each awakened a desire the beautiful thing itself could not satisfy. 'If I find in myself a desire which no experience in this world can satisfy, the most probable explanation is that I was made for another world.' Dostoevsky, writing from the opposite direction, has a character say 'Beauty will save the world.' Both were pointing at the same thing.
practice_q: Without looking: what did Lewis mean by 'Joy', and why doesn't sexual selection explain why humans weep at Bach?
convo: When a piece of music or a sunset stops you in your tracks, does it ever feel like it's pointing at something more? What do you think that is?

title: The Argument from Religious Experience
tagline: Billions of people across all cultures report direct experience of God
p1: The experience: billions of people across all cultures and centuries report direct experiences of God — presence, holiness, being known and loved.
p2: The principle: in ordinary life we treat perceptual reports as evidence for what they report, unless we have strong reason to doubt them.
p3: The best explanation: with no strong universal reason to dismiss them all, religious experience provides genuine evidence for God.
evidence: Richard Swinburne argues that the standards we use for ordinary experience — trusting a thousand independent reports of a meteor shower — apply here too. The core phenomenology is strikingly consistent across traditions, which is what you'd expect if people were encountering a real transcendent reality; though who God truly is, the Christian holds, is settled by his self-revelation in Christ and Scripture, not by experience alone. Neural correlates disprove nothing — every experience has them.
practice_q: Without looking: why doesn't 'it's just brain chemistry' refute religious experience — and what core features do reports across different religions actually share?
convo: Have you — or anyone you trust — ever had a moment that felt like a genuine encounter with something beyond the physical?

title: The Argument from Desire
tagline: Our deepest longings point toward a reality that can satisfy them
p1: The experience: humans universally feel a longing for ultimate meaning, love, and belonging that no earthly experience fully satisfies.
p2: The pattern: every natural desire points to a real satisfaction — hunger to food, loneliness to people, curiosity to knowledge.
p3: The best explanation: this deepest longing points toward a reality beyond the earthly that can satisfy it — Christians identify that reality as God.
evidence: Pascal spoke of the emptiness that wealth, pleasure, success, and relationships all fail to fill; Augustine put it better: 'Our heart is restless until it rests in Thee.' Every human civilisation in history has reached toward the transcendent. Nor is this wishful thinking — Lewis felt the longing as an atheist and resisted where it pointed. On atheism it is a cruel cosmic joke; on theism, a compass pointing home.
practice_q: Without looking: how does this argument answer 'isn't that just wishful thinking?' — and what was Lewis's own starting point?
convo: Have you ever finally gotten the thing you wanted most and still felt a quiet 'is that all?' — what do you think that longing is really for?

title: The Problem of Evil as Evidence for God
tagline: The very fact that evil feels objectively wrong presupposes an objective good
p1: People raise the problem of evil because they believe some things are genuinely, objectively evil.
p2: 'Objectively evil' presupposes an objective moral standard — one that cannot be grounded in human preference.
p3: So the very premise of the problem of evil points toward God, the ground of that standard.
evidence: Concede the mystery first: evil and suffering are a genuine difficulty for theism, and no tidy theodicy dissolves them — Christians from Augustine to Alvin Plantinga have answered seriously, Plantinga's free-will defense showing consistency, not offering proof. Yet calling the Holocaust objectively evil — not merely unpleasant — smuggles in a moral framework that makes sense only if an objective standard, and its ground, is real.
practice_q: Without looking: what must you concede first when someone raises the problem of evil — and what hidden presupposition does the objection itself carry?
convo: When something terrible happens and we call it truly evil — not just sad — where do you think that standard of 'evil' comes from?

title: The Minimal Facts Argument
tagline: Start where sceptical scholars already agree — then ask which explanation fits every fact
p1: A short list of facts commands assent across the scholarly spectrum — atheist, agnostic, and Jewish scholars included: Jesus died by crucifixion; his disciples sincerely believed he appeared to them alive; the persecutor Paul and the sceptical brother James were converted by what they took to be appearances of the risen Jesus.
p2: Every naturalistic theory — hallucination, stolen body, swoon, legend — explains at most a fact or two and breaks on the rest; the early 1 Corinthians 15 creed alone closes the door on legend.
p3: The resurrection is the one explanation that accounts for the whole set at once — what Michael Licona carefully calls the 'best historical explanation' of the evidence, not a knockdown proof.
evidence: Gary Habermas surveyed over 3,400 scholarly sources to identify facts commanding near-universal assent — sceptics included. Jesus's crucifixion is confirmed by Josephus and Tacitus; even the radical sceptic John Dominic Crossan calls it 'as sure as anything historical can ever be.' The 1 Corinthians 15:3–7 creed, dated across the spectrum to within roughly 2–5 years of the crucifixion, leaves no time for legend. The empty tomb is accepted by a majority of historians, though it is the most disputed of the facts.
practice_q: Without looking: name the minimal facts Habermas builds on, and explain why the 1 Corinthians 15:3–7 creed — dated within roughly 2–5 years of the crucifixion — rules out slow-growing legend.
convo: If we set aside the Bible's inspiration entirely and used only the facts sceptical historians already grant, what do you think best explains them all together?

title: The Empty Tomb
tagline: His enemies explained the emptiness instead of denying it
p1: Jesus was buried in a known, identifiable tomb belonging to a named man, Joseph of Arimathea — so the claim 'the tomb is empty' could be checked, in Jerusalem, within weeks of the events.
p2: The accounts carry the marks of genuine memory — women as the first witnesses in a culture that discounted their testimony — and the authorities' own counter-story, 'the disciples stole the body,' concedes the tomb was empty.
p3: An empty tomb alone does not prove a resurrection — but it rules out every theory that leaves the body in place, and joined to the appearance reports it forms a cumulative case.
evidence: The creed Paul 'received' — that Christ died, was buried, and was raised (1 Corinthians 15:3–4) — traces to within a few years of the events and was proclaimed in Jerusalem, where anyone could walk to the tomb. All four Gospels name women as the first witnesses, testimony their culture dismissed. And the earliest counter-story, that the disciples stole the body (Matthew 28:13), was still circulating in Justin Martyr's and Tertullian's day — an explanation that concedes the emptiness it tries to explain.
practice_q: Without looking: explain why the 'disciples stole the body' charge — recorded in Matthew 28 and still repeated in Justin Martyr's time — actually concedes that the tomb was empty.
convo: Have you ever noticed that the earliest counter-explanation wasn't 'the body is still there' but 'someone took it'? What do you make of that?

title: Paul's Conversion and the Early Creed
tagline: The church's persecutor, carrying a creed dated within years of the crucifixion
p1: Paul was the church's active enemy — 'I persecuted the church of God violently and tried to destroy it' (Galatians 1:13) — with no grief, no prior expectation, and every reason to stay hostile.
p2: He received and handed on the 1 Corinthians 15:3–7 creed — dated across the scholarly spectrum to within roughly 2–5 years of the crucifixion — and checked it in person with Peter and James in Jerusalem (Galatians 1:18–19).
p3: His sincerity alone does not prove the resurrection; what it removes is grief, wishful thinking, and slow legend — leaving his own explanation, an encounter with the risen Jesus, as the strongest one standing.
evidence: Paul persecuted the church 'violently' by his own admission (Galatians 1:13). Three years after his conversion he spent fifteen days with Peter and met James (Galatians 1:18–19), receiving the creed from eyewitnesses within roughly 3–8 years of the crucifixion. The atheist scholar Gerd Lüdemann dates it to 'two to three years after the death of Jesus'; James D.G. Dunn argues it was formulated within months of Jesus' death. Paul adds that most of the 500-plus witnesses were still living — an open invitation to verify. Legends need generations; this had almost none.
practice_q: Without looking: reconstruct the Galatians 1 timeline that lets scholars — including the sceptic Gerd Lüdemann — date the 1 Corinthians 15 creed to within a few years of the crucifixion.
convo: Why do you think a man who was arresting Christians would suddenly give up his status and safety to join them?

title: The Resurrection Appearances
tagline: Individuals and crowds, friends, a doubter, and an enemy — all said they saw him alive
p1: The earliest testimony — the 1 Corinthians 15:3–7 creed, dated within roughly 2–5 years of the crucifixion — is a roll call of witnesses: Peter, the Twelve, more than 500 at once, James, all the apostles, and Paul.
p2: Four independent streams — Paul, Mark, Luke, John — describe bodily, interactive encounters, and the witnesses range from devoted friends to a sceptical brother and a hostile persecutor.
p3: Hallucinations are private experiences — there are no well-documented cases of shared group hallucinations — and neither Paul nor James was grieving — real appearances best explain the full range of independent reports.
evidence: The 1 Corinthians 15:3–7 creed, dated within roughly 2–5 years of the crucifixion, lists appearances to Peter, the Twelve, more than 500 at once, James, all the apostles, and Paul — most of the 500, Paul notes, still living to be questioned. Four independent traditions — Paul, Mark, Luke, John — converge on bodily encounters: Jesus eats fish, shows his wounds, cooks breakfast. Hallucinations are private experiences — there are no well-documented cases of shared group hallucinations — and Paul and James were not grieving disciples.
practice_q: Without looking: list the witnesses named in the 1 Corinthians 15:3–7 creed, and give two reasons the hallucination theory struggles to cover them all.
convo: One grieving friend might imagine seeing someone they loved again — but a doubting brother and a hostile persecutor too? What do you think happened to them?

title: The Origin of the Disciples' Belief
tagline: Something happened that turned frightened followers into fearless witnesses
p1: After the crucifixion the disciples fled, denied, and hid; within fifty days they were publicly proclaiming the resurrection in Jerusalem — the one city where the claim could most easily be checked.
p2: What they proclaimed had no category in Jewish expectation — resurrection hope was bodily, universal, and at history's end, not one man raised in the middle of history (N.T. Wright's theological novelty argument).
p3: Their willingness to suffer and die proves they truly believed it — sincerity is not truth, but it rules out fraud — and a genuine encounter with the risen Jesus best explains how such a belief ever arose at all.
evidence: Peter denied Jesus three times and ran; the disciples hid behind locked doors. Within seven weeks they were proclaiming the resurrection publicly in Jerusalem, within walking distance of the tomb. Wright shows their claim — one man raised bodily in the middle of history — had no slot in Jewish expectation waiting to be filled. Martyrdom is firmly attested for Peter, Paul, and James the brother of Jesus; the argument doesn't need 'all twelve' — they faced lethal danger and did not recant.
practice_q: Without looking: state N.T. Wright's theological novelty point — the three ways first-century Jewish resurrection hope differed from what the disciples actually proclaimed.
convo: What do you think could change a man who denied Jesus in front of a servant girl into one who wouldn't recant under threat of death?

title: The Conversion of the Sceptics
tagline: The people least likely to believe became the most committed believers
p1: Paul was an active enemy who persecuted the church; James, Jesus's own brother, did not believe in him during his ministry (Mark 3:21; John 7:5).
p2: Both claimed the risen Jesus appeared to them — James is named in the early 1 Corinthians 15 creed, dated within a handful of years of the events — and both led the early church and held that conviction to violent deaths.
p3: Their deaths prove sincere belief, not automatically the event itself — but sincere resurrection belief in a hostile persecutor and a sceptical brother is precisely what grief and wish-fulfilment cannot produce.
evidence: Mark 3:21 records that Jesus's family thought him out of his mind; John 7:5 says plainly that his brothers did not believe in him. Yet the early creed names James among those the risen Jesus appeared to (1 Corinthians 15:7), and Josephus — no Christian — records his stoning in Jerusalem around AD 62. Paul, the church's persecutor, was put to death in Rome by strong early tradition. Neither man fits the grief-hallucination profile: neither was mourning a beloved teacher.
practice_q: Without looking: explain why grief-hallucination theories fail for Paul and James specifically, and what the church's preserved record of James's earlier doubt suggests about the accounts.
convo: What would it take to convince you that your own executed brother was the risen Lord? Something changed James — what do you think it was?

title: The Argument from the Burial
tagline: Before there can be an empty tomb, there has to be a known tomb
p1: Jesus was buried by Joseph of Arimathea — a named member of the very council that condemned him — attested in all four Gospels and in the early creed's 'he was buried' (1 Corinthians 15:4).
p2: No inventor would choose a Sanhedrin member as the hero, or women as the witnesses to where he was laid — the criterion of embarrassment — and Jewish burial law and practice make the account historically expected.
p3: The burial does not prove the resurrection; it establishes a known, checkable location — turning 'the tomb is empty' into a public, falsifiable claim rather than a private feeling.
evidence: All four Gospels name Joseph of Arimathea — a member of the very council that condemned Jesus — and the early creed itself confesses 'he was buried' (1 Corinthians 15:4). Raymond Brown, a critical Catholic scholar and no apologist, judged the burial among the best attested facts about Jesus. Jewish law required same-day burial (Deuteronomy 21:22–23), Josephus confirms the practice, and the 1968 Yehohanan find — a crucified man properly buried, the nail still in his heel — matches the Gospel picture.
practice_q: Without looking: give two criterion-of-embarrassment details in the burial account, and explain what the 1968 Yehohanan discovery contributes to its plausibility.
convo: Have you noticed the resurrection was a checkable claim — a named man's tomb at a known location, announced in the same city? Why launch a movement on a claim like that?

title: The Argument from Post-Resurrection Behaviour
tagline: The disciples behaved exactly as you would expect if the resurrection was real
p1: Within weeks, frightened deserters were proclaiming the resurrection in Jerusalem — the worst possible place to fabricate it — and maintained that testimony under flogging and execution.
p2: Their entire pattern of life changed: devout Jewish monotheists began worshipping the crucified Jesus, honouring the first day of the week — the day of the resurrection — and building new rites around his death and rising.
p3: Their courage shows they were not knowing liars; the remaining question is what produced such belief so early and so completely — and meeting the risen Jesus explains the whole web of consequences at once.
evidence: Paul's letters from the AD 50s already show Jewish monotheists praying to Jesus and applying to him words their Scriptures reserved for God (Philippians 2; Romans 10:13) — Larry Hurtado's 'devotional explosion' within twenty years. They honoured the first day of the week (1 Corinthians 16:2; Acts 20:7; Ignatius, c. 107 AD) and re-enacted the Lord's Supper as carefully received tradition (1 Corinthians 11). Communities do not build a joyful, repeated meal around the death of a leader they believe simply failed.
practice_q: Without looking: name three behavioural changes in the earliest Jewish Christians and explain why each is hard to account for without genuine resurrection belief.
convo: What do you think could move devout Jewish monotheists — for whom idolatry was the gravest possible sin — to worship a recently crucified man?

title: The Historical Existence of Jesus
tagline: Five independent streams of ancient evidence — including hostile witnesses
p1: Jesus is attested by five independent streams of evidence: Tacitus, Josephus, Pliny the Younger, Paul's letters (c. 50-60 AD), and the Gospels.
p2: Two of these — Tacitus and Josephus — are hostile or non-Christian witnesses with no motive to support the Christian movement; when an enemy confirms a fact, it carries the most weight.
p3: By the ordinary standards historians apply to ancient figures, a real Jesus of Nazareth who lived, taught, and was crucified under Pontius Pilate is among the most secure facts in ancient history.
evidence: Tacitus (Annals 15.44, c. 116 AD) records the execution of 'Christus' under Pontius Pilate; Josephus (c. 93 AD) mentions Jesus and 'James the brother of Jesus who was called Christ'; Paul, writing within 20 years of the crucifixion, personally knew Peter and James (Galatians 1:18-19). Even the agnostic scholar Bart Ehrman insists Jesus 'certainly existed' — mythicism is rejected by historians of every persuasion.
practice_q: Without looking: name the five independent streams of evidence for Jesus's existence, and explain why the hostile witnesses count for the most.
convo: Have you ever wondered what historians outside the Bible — even ones who disliked Christianity — actually said about Jesus?

title: Jesus as God in the New Testament
tagline: From Paul's earliest letters to Revelation, every corner of the New Testament places Jesus within the divine identity
p1: The first Christians were strict Jewish monotheists — yet from the very beginning they worshipped Jesus alongside God the Father: prayer, hymns, baptism in his name.
p2: Paul's letters (c. 50-60 AD) already apply YHWH-texts to Jesus (1 Corinthians 8:6; Philippians 2:9-11; Romans 10:13) — and quote hymns and creeds older than the letters themselves.
p3: John, Hebrews, and Revelation confirm the same claim with careful precision: the Son is personally distinct from the Father, yet fully God in nature. The divine identity of Jesus is Christianity's starting point, not a late development.
evidence: Paul rewrites the Shema around Jesus in 1 Corinthians 8:6 and applies Isaiah 45:23 — every knee bowing to YHWH — to Jesus in Philippians 2. Hebrews has the Father command, 'Let all God's angels worship him' (1:6). Revelation gives Jesus God's exclusive title, 'the Alpha and the Omega' (22:13; cf. Isaiah 44:6). Larry Hurtado called this devotion a 'big bang' erupting within the earliest years after the resurrection.
practice_q: Without looking: how does Paul's rewriting of the Shema in 1 Corinthians 8:6 show that full divine Christology predates John's Gospel by decades?
convo: What do you think could convince first-century Jewish monotheists — people who would rather die than worship a creature — to worship a crucified man as Lord?

title: The HANDS Framework
tagline: Honors, Attributes, Names, Deeds, Seat — five lines of divine identity converging on Jesus
p1: In Jewish monotheism five things belong to God alone: the honors of worship, the attributes of eternity, the divine names, the deeds of creating and judging, and the seat of God's throne.
p2: The New Testament — written by monotheistic Jews — deliberately gives Jesus all five, across multiple independent authors.
p3: One verse can be argued over; five independent lines converging on one man is an identification. Run the test on Moses, Enoch, or Michael: no other figure satisfies even two, and the worship line fails every time.
evidence: Jesus receives the worship angels refuse (Revelation 5:13 vs. 19:10); claims 'Before Abraham was, I AM' (John 8:58); bears YHWH-texts as Kyrios (Romans 10:13); creates and upholds all things (John 1:3; Hebrews 1:3); and shares the single 'throne of God and of the Lamb' (Revelation 22:1-3). The framework comes from Bowman and Komoszewski's study Putting Jesus in His Place.
practice_q: Without looking: recite the five letters of HANDS with one supporting text for each — then explain why the convergence matters more than any single verse.
convo: If you listed the things ancient Judaism said belong to God alone, would it surprise you that the New Testament quietly hands every one of them to Jesus?

title: Did Jesus Claim to Be God?
tagline: Jesus made divine claims his Jewish audience understood clearly — and they tried to stone him for it
p1: Jesus quietly assumed the prerogatives of God: forgiving sins (Mark 2), lordship over the Sabbath, the absolute 'I AM' (John 8:58), and Daniel 7's throne at his trial (Mark 14:62).
p2: His audience understood him perfectly — three attempts to stone him for blasphemy in John's Gospel, and a blasphemy conviction before the high priest. Claiming to be a merely human Messiah tore no robes.
p3: As C.S. Lewis argued, 'great moral teacher' is the one option not available: a merely human Jesus who said these things would be a liar or deluded — and his character and resurrection rule both out, leaving Lord.
evidence: In Mark 2 the scribes ask, 'Who can forgive sins but God alone?' — and Jesus heals the paralytic precisely to prove his authority. At his trial he fuses Psalm 110 and Daniel 7, claiming the seat beside God's throne; the high priest tears his robes. The blasphemy charge is his enemies' own testimony that they heard a claim far beyond messiahship.
practice_q: Without looking: give three divine prerogatives Jesus assumed, and explain why his trial verdict shows his contemporaries understood him to be claiming more than messiahship.
convo: People often say Jesus never claimed to be God. Would you be curious to look at why his own enemies charged him with blasphemy?

title: Messianic Prophecy — Foretold and Fulfilled
tagline: Specific prophecies written centuries before Jesus converge on one person
p1: The Old Testament gives specific details about the coming Messiah: his birthplace (Micah 5:2), his suffering and burial (Isaiah 53), the manner of his death (Psalm 22), even the betrayal price (Zechariah 11:12-13).
p2: The Dead Sea Scrolls — including the Great Isaiah Scroll, c. 125 BC — prove these texts predate Jesus by more than a century; nothing was written after the fact.
p3: The strongest fulfilments — birthplace, lineage, manner and timing of death — were outside anyone's power to arrange. No single line is a mathematical proof; the pointing power lies in how many independent details converge on one person.
evidence: Isaiah 53 (c. 700 BC) describes a servant pierced for our transgressions, silent before his accusers, assigned a grave with the wicked yet buried with the rich, and alive after his suffering. Psalm 22 describes pierced hands and feet and lots cast for clothing — written a millennium before Roman crucifixion. The Great Isaiah Scroll proves the promises were on parchment before Jesus was born.
practice_q: Without looking: name three messianic prophecies fulfilled in details Jesus could not have controlled, and explain how the Dead Sea Scrolls rule out after-the-fact writing.
convo: If documents provably older than Jesus described his birthplace, his death, and his burial — how would you weigh that convergence?

title: John 1:1 and the Pre-existence of Christ
tagline: The Word was God — what the Greek actually says
p1: 'In the beginning was the Word' — the Word already existed when the beginning began (the imperfect 'was,' against the 'came to be' used for created things in 1:3).
p2: 'The Word was with God' — personally distinct from the Father; 'the Word was God' — fully sharing the divine nature, the qualitative sense of the Greek. Distinct person, one divine nature.
p3: 'The Word became flesh' (John 1:14): the eternal Word, the agent of all creation, became the man Jesus of Nazareth — fully God and fully human.
evidence: John deliberately echoes Genesis 1:1, placing Jesus at creation: 'through him all things were made' (John 1:3). Greek grammarian Daniel B. Wallace calls John's construction the most concise way to say the Logos was God and yet distinct from the Father. The Jehovah's Witness rendering 'a god' is grammatically unjustified — and a subordinate god could not be the agent through whom all things were made.
practice_q: Without looking: explain how John 1:1's three clauses keep the Word distinct from the Father yet fully God — and why 'a god' fails on John's own logic in verse 3.
convo: Most biographies start with a birth. John starts before time itself — 'In the beginning was the Word.' What do you make of a claim that size?

title: The Philippians 2 Hymn
tagline: An early Christian hymn treating Jesus as fully divine — within years of the crucifixion
p1: Philippians 2:6-11 is a pre-Pauline hymn: Paul quotes it as existing tradition, so it is older than his letter — most scholars date it within 20 years of the crucifixion.
p2: It describes Christ existing 'in the form of God' before his incarnation, who 'emptied himself' by taking the form of a servant — adding our humanity, never subtracting his deity. He remained what he was, and became what he was not.
p3: It climaxes by applying Isaiah 45:23 — every knee bowing to YHWH alone — to Jesus, 'to the glory of God the Father.' The divine Jesus was being sung long before Nicaea.
evidence: The hymn uses the same word morphe — essential form — for both 'form of God' and 'form of a servant': he did not trade one nature for another. The 'name above every name' is the divine name itself, and the every-knee confession comes from Isaiah 45, the Old Testament's fiercest one-God chapter. Christians were singing this while eyewitnesses of Jesus were still alive.
practice_q: Without looking: what does 'he emptied himself' mean — and not mean — in Philippians 2, and why does applying Isaiah 45:23 to Jesus place him within the identity of the one God?
convo: Did you know the earliest Christian song we possess — older than most of the New Testament — already gives Jesus the worship Isaiah reserves for God alone?

title: The Titles of Jesus
tagline: Son of Man, Lord, I AM, Son of God — what they meant to a first-century Jewish audience
p1: Each title carried specific freight in first-century Judaism: 'Son of Man' evokes Daniel 7's figure who comes with the clouds — God's own mode of arrival — and receives everlasting dominion and the service of all peoples.
p2: 'Lord' (Kyrios) translates the divine name YHWH in the Greek Old Testament, and the New Testament applies YHWH-passages directly to Jesus (Romans 10:13); 'I AM' echoes God's self-revelation in Exodus 3:14; 'Son of God' claims a unique mutual knowledge with the Father (Matthew 11:27).
p3: His hearers' reactions — stones picked up, robes torn, a blasphemy charge — confirm they understood the divine weight of these titles exactly.
evidence: 'Son of Man' sounds humble but points to Daniel 7:13-14, where one coming 'with the clouds of heaven' receives everlasting dominion and the worship of all nations. When Jesus claimed that identity at his trial (Mark 14:62), the high priest tore his robes. The risen Jesus even takes Isaiah's exclusive self-title: 'I am the First and the Last' (Revelation 1:17; cf. Isaiah 44:6).
practice_q: Without looking: for each of the four titles — Son of Man, Lord, I AM, Son of God — give the Old Testament background that makes it a divine claim in context.
convo: 'Son of Man' sounds like the humblest title Jesus could have picked. Would it surprise you that it may be the most exalted?

title: The Criterion of Multiple Attestation
tagline: Historical methodology confirms key facts about Jesus across independent sources
p1: Historians trust a fact more when it appears in multiple sources that did not copy from each other — the same tool used for any ancient battle, king, or plague.
p2: The crucifixion is attested in six independent streams: Paul, Mark, non-Markan Synoptic material, John, Josephus, and Tacitus. Jesus's baptism by John and his fame as a healer are likewise multiply attested.
p3: Honestly stated, the criterion does not prove a miracle by itself — but it makes the broad outline of Jesus's life (teacher, healer, crucified under Pilate) impossible to dismiss as later legend.
evidence: Even the radical sceptic John Dominic Crossan concedes: 'That Jesus was crucified is as sure as anything historical can ever be.' The baptism by John appears in Mark, Q, John, and Josephus; even Jesus's opponents explained his deeds rather than denying them (Matthew 12:24). E.P. Sanders built his whole portrait of Jesus on exactly these multiply attested facts.
practice_q: Without looking: define multiple attestation, list the six independent streams attesting the crucifixion, and state honestly what the criterion does not prove.
convo: When several witnesses who never compared notes tell the same story, what do you usually conclude? That's the very tool historians apply to Jesus.

title: The Early Creed of 1 Corinthians 15
tagline: The resurrection was proclaimed within years of the crucifixion
p1: Paul 'delivered' what he 'received' — formal rabbinic transmission language: 1 Corinthians 15:3-7 is a creed older than the letter that contains it.
p2: Counting back through Paul's conversion and his Jerusalem visit to Peter and James (Galatians 1:18-19), scholars — including the atheist Gerd Lüdemann — date the creed to within roughly 2-5 years of the crucifixion.
p3: It names living, checkable witnesses — Cephas, the Twelve, more than 500 at once, James, all the apostles. There is no time for legend; the resurrection claim is bedrock, and it is early.
evidence: The creed proclaims that Christ died for our sins, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared to named witnesses. Paul adds that most of the 500 'are still living' — an open invitation to verify. James D. G. Dunn writes: 'This tradition, we can be entirely confident, was formulated as tradition within months of Jesus' death.' Legends need generations to displace eyewitness memory; this claim was fixed within a handful of years.
practice_q: Without looking: reconstruct the four lines of the 1 Corinthians 15 creed and the chain of dating that places it within a few years of the cross.
convo: What if the resurrection claim wasn't a legend that grew over centuries — but a creed recited within a few years, naming witnesses you could go and ask?

title: The Argument from Jesus's Character
tagline: A moral character without historical parallel — and what it points to
p1: Jesus's moral vision — love of enemies, unlimited forgiveness, radical humility, care for the despised — was without precedent in the ancient world, and he lived it: he died forgiving his executioners (Luke 23:34).
p2: The same manifestly good and sane man made towering claims — forgiving sins, 'Before Abraham was, I AM' — and the goodness and the claims come bundled in the same person.
p3: That combination closes the comfortable middle option: as C.S. Lewis observed, a merely human being who said these things would not be a great moral teacher. This argument doesn't prove divinity alone — it makes 'liar' and 'lunatic' ring false.
evidence: The Gospels preserve details no inventor of a perfect hero would choose — Jesus's family thinking him mad (Mark 3:21), his anguish in Gethsemane, his cry of dereliction. Lewis, a professional literary critic, judged: 'The Gospels are not like' the legends and myths he had read all his life. The character is seamless under the hardest test — teaching enemy-love, then praying forgiveness over the men crucifying him.
practice_q: Without looking: explain why 'great moral teacher, nothing more' is not an available verdict on Jesus — and what this argument honestly does not prove by itself.
convo: Even people who reject Christianity keep reaching for Jesus's words when they want to describe goodness. Where do you think a character like that came from?

title: Daniel's 70 Weeks
tagline: A timeline written centuries before Jesus points to his era — and the sequence is the striking part
p1: Daniel 9:24-27 lays out seventy 'sevens' of years from a decree to restore Jerusalem to an anointed one — mashiach — who comes and is 'cut off,' a violent death.
p2: The prophecy fixes a sequence: the anointed one comes, is killed, and afterward the city and sanctuary are destroyed — and Jerusalem's temple fell in AD 70, decades after the crucifixion.
p3: Even granting the critical second-century BC dating of Daniel, the text still anticipates an anointed one cut off before the temple's fall — and Jesus fits that closing window. Offer it as a striking, pondering-worthy pattern, never as arithmetic proof.
evidence: Dead Sea Scroll fragments of Daniel (c. 120 BC) and the Septuagint prove the prophecy predates Jesus by at least 150 years. Sir Robert Anderson's famous day-count from the 444 BC decree of Artaxerxes is one influential interpretation, not a scholarly consensus — the honest case says so plainly. The durable point is the sequence: an anointed one 'cut off,' then Jerusalem destroyed, matched in AD 30-70.
practice_q: Without looking: what is the fragile part of the Daniel 9 argument, what is the durable part, and why does the durable part survive even a second-century date for Daniel?
convo: Here's a puzzle worth sitting with: an ancient text expects an anointed one to be killed before the temple falls — and the temple fell in AD 70. Who fits that window?

title: The Uniqueness of the Resurrection Claim
tagline: No other religion makes a falsifiable historical claim of this kind
p1: Christianity's central claim is a specific, public, historical event: one man, in one place, at one time, risen bodily from the dead.
p2: The claim is falsifiable in principle — Paul himself staked everything on it: 'If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile' (1 Corinthians 15:17).
p3: No other major religion stands or falls on a checkable event — and the claim broke the disciples' own Jewish categories, which expected resurrection for everyone at the end of history, not one man in the middle of it. A faith willing to be checked is a faith worth checking.
evidence: Buddhism's teachings stand whether or not the Buddha lived; Islam's central miracle is a book, not an investigable event. The resurrection was proclaimed within a few years, in Jerusalem — the one city where producing the body could have crushed it — naming living witnesses, including converted sceptics like Paul and James. Being unprecedented doesn't prove it true; it does mean the claim invites investigation.
practice_q: Without looking: what makes Christianity's central claim epistemically unique among world religions — and what does that uniqueness show and not show?
convo: Most faiths ask you to accept a teaching. Christianity dares you to investigate an event. Does that difference matter to you?

title: The Virgin Birth and Isaiah 7:14
tagline: Two independent traditions — and a prophecy Jewish translators heard before Christianity existed
p1: Matthew (through Joseph's eyes) and Luke (through Mary's) report the virginal conception independently — almost no shared wording, opposite vantage points, the same astonishing core.
p2: Two independent sources converging on one central fact is the historian's fingerprint of a shared early tradition, not a single invented story — and a pregnancy before marriage was an embarrassing claim no church would fabricate.
p3: Isaiah 7:14 is the scriptural resonance Matthew heard, not the load-bearing proof: Jewish Septuagint translators chose parthenos — virgin — around 200 BC, before Christianity existed. Theologically, the virgin birth guards both truths: born of Mary, truly human; conceived by the Spirit, God's own initiative.
evidence: Matthew and Luke share only the essentials — Mary, Joseph, Bethlehem, conception by the Holy Spirit, the name Jesus — and differ in nearly every surrounding detail, ruling out copying. The claim invited the ugliest rumors in that culture, which no inventor would choose. By around 107 AD, Ignatius of Antioch already treats Mary's virginity as settled teaching the church proclaimed.
practice_q: Without looking: why does the independence of Matthew's and Luke's infancy accounts carry more historical weight than the almah/parthenos debate — and what two truths does the virgin birth guard?
convo: If two witnesses who clearly never compared notes agree on one astonishing fact, how would you explain the agreement?

title: The Argument from the Resurrection Predictions
tagline: Jesus predicted his own death and resurrection — and was right
p1: Jesus predicted his death and resurrection three times, with increasing specificity (Mark 8:31; 9:31; 10:33-34) — betrayal, condemnation, Gentile execution, and rising on the third day.
p2: The disciples consistently failed to understand: Peter rebuked him, the Twelve argued about greatness, James and John asked for seats of honour — and all fled at the arrest. That embarrassing obliviousness is the mark of genuine memory, not prophecy written backwards.
p3: A foreseen death is a purpose, not a tragedy: Jesus walked to Jerusalem with open eyes, interpreting his death in advance as 'a ransom for many' (Mark 10:45) and trusting God's vindication beyond it.
evidence: If the church had invented the predictions after Easter, it would have portrayed the disciples as understanding and prepared. Instead they scatter at the arrest and treat the first resurrection reports as nonsense — the criterion of embarrassment at full force. And the details exceed political foresight: crucifixion rather than stoning, betrayal by a disciple, and rising 'on the third day.'
practice_q: Without looking: how does the disciples' repeated failure to understand the passion predictions answer the objection that they were inserted after the resurrection?
convo: If a friend calmly told you he would be executed and rise three days later — and then it happened — what would you do with that?

title: The Manuscript Reliability Argument
tagline: The New Testament is the most well-attested document in ancient history
p1: The New Testament survives in over 5,800 Greek manuscripts — more than any other ancient document — with the earliest fragments within a generation or so of composition.
p2: Because copies fanned out early across Egypt, Syria, Rome, and Asia Minor, comparing the many independent witnesses lets scholars identify slips and recover the original text — abundance is an asset, not a liability.
p3: Therefore the New Testament text has been accurately preserved. That is a claim about the copying, not a proof that the content is true — whether the events happened is argued separately, on its own evidence.
evidence: Over 5,800 Greek manuscripts survive, plus over 19,000 in other languages — Caesar's Gallic Wars has 10, Plato's Republic 7. The John Rylands Papyrus (P52), a fragment of John, dates to roughly 117-138 AD, within about 40 years of composition. Of some 400,000 variants, the vast majority are trivial; Daniel Wallace estimates the text is stable in well over 99% of its wording, and Bruce Metzger concluded no major doctrine is affected by any variant.
practice_q: Without looking: how many Greek manuscripts of the New Testament survive, how does that compare with Caesar's Gallic Wars — and what exactly does this evidence establish, and what does it deliberately not establish?
convo: If historians trust the text of Caesar and Plato from a handful of copies made centuries later, what would be fair to conclude about a text with over 5,800 copies, some within a generation?

title: Archaeological Confirmation of the Bible
tagline: Hundreds of archaeological discoveries confirm biblical people, places, and details
p1: Critics repeatedly declared biblical people and places legendary — the Hittites, Pontius Pilate, the Pool of Bethesda, even the House of David.
p2: Excavation then confirmed each: Hattusa and the Hittite empire (1906), the Pilate Stone with the historically correct title "prefect" (1961), the five-colonnade pool John described, the Caiaphas ossuary (1990), and the Tel Dan "House of David" inscription (1993).
p3: Archaeology corroborates the setting and incidental details — showing the writers knew their world as insiders, not legend-makers — but it cannot dig up a miracle or a doctrine; both halves of that sentence are true and worth holding honestly.
evidence: The Hittites, dismissed as legend, were confirmed when Hattusa was excavated in 1906. The Pilate Stone (Caesarea, 1961) names Pontius Pilate with the correct title "Prefect of Judaea" — a term later writers got wrong. The Caiaphas ossuary (1990) and the five-colonnade Pool of Bethesda match Gospel details. William Albright: "Discovery after discovery has established the accuracy of innumerable details, and has brought increased recognition of the Bible as a source of history."
practice_q: Without looking: name three discoveries that overturned a claim that a biblical person or place was invented — and state precisely what archaeology can show about the Bible, and what it cannot.
convo: Time and again a biblical person was called legendary and then dug up — Pilate, the Hittites, the pool with five colonnades. At what point does that track record earn the text a serious hearing?

title: The Canon Formation Argument
tagline: The books of the Bible were recognised, not invented, by the church
p1: The Council of Nicaea (325 AD) never discussed which books belong in the Bible — it addressed the Arian controversy about Christ's deity; no ancient source records any canon vote there.
p2: The core New Testament — the four Gospels, Acts, Paul's letters — was already universally recognised by around 180 AD, weighed on principled criteria: apostolic origin, universal use across the churches, and consistency with the apostolic faith.
p3: So the church recognised books that already carried authority — like an assayer identifying gold rather than making it. The Gnostic gospels failed those criteria because they were late and not apostolic, not for political reasons. On these twenty-seven books Catholics, Orthodox, and Protestants stand together; the old, gracious family conversation about a few books at the edges of the Old Testament leaves this story untouched.
evidence: Nicaea (325 AD) addressed the Arian controversy; no ancient source records it discussing the canon. Irenaeus (c. 180 AD) treats the four Gospels as settled: "It is not possible that the Gospels can be either more or fewer in number than they are." The Muratorian Fragment (c. 170-200 AD) already lists nearly our New Testament. Even Bart Ehrman agrees the Gnostic gospels "were never part of the canon in the first place."
practice_q: Without looking: what did the Council of Nicaea actually debate, what three criteria did the early church use to recognise canonical books, and how early does Irenaeus treat the four Gospels as settled?
convo: Many people have heard that an emperor picked the books of the Bible at Nicaea. Would it surprise you to learn that no record of that council so much as mentions the canon?

title: The Argument from Fulfilled Prophecy in the Old Testament
tagline: The Old Testament accurately predicted events centuries before they occurred
p1: The Old Testament contains specific predictions about nations, cities, and events — Tyre's fall (Ezekiel 26), Cyrus named as Israel's deliverer (Isaiah 44-45), the seventy-year exile and return (Jeremiah 29:10) — in texts documented before the events.
p2: Many were fulfilled in verifiable historical detail, sometimes across events no writer could arrange — Ezekiel's Tyre prophecy required two separate conquests 240 years apart to fulfil.
p3: Everything hangs on the word "before": where the dating is secure — and the Dead Sea Scrolls settle it for key texts — the pattern is best explained by divine inspiration, while honest evaluation gladly leans on the strong cases and acknowledges genuine scholarly debate about others.
evidence: Ezekiel 26 (c. 590 BC) predicted Tyre's stones and timber would be thrown into the sea. Nebuchadnezzar destroyed mainland Tyre by 573 BC; in 332 BC Alexander scraped its rubble into the sea to build his causeway — two conquests, 240 years apart. Cyrus, named in Isaiah 44-45, freed the exiles after taking Babylon in 539 BC — a policy confirmed by the Cyrus Cylinder. The Dead Sea Scrolls establish key prophetic texts predate the events by centuries.
practice_q: Without looking: reconstruct the Tyre prophecy — what did Ezekiel predict, which two conquerors fulfilled it, how far apart — and explain why the whole argument rises or falls on the prophecy predating the event.
convo: Ezekiel said Tyre's stones and timber would end up in the sea — and 240 years after Babylon, Alexander built his causeway out of exactly that rubble. What would it take for a prediction like that to impress you?

title: The Internal Consistency of Scripture
tagline: 66 books written across 1,500 years by 40 authors telling one coherent story
p1: The Bible's 66 books were written by roughly 40 authors — kings, shepherds, fishermen, a physician, a Rabbinically trained scholar — across about 1,500 years, in three languages, on three continents, with no editorial board coordinating them.
p2: Yet it tells one unfolding story — creation, fall, promise, redemption, new creation — with covenant and typological threads planted in Genesis (the Passover lamb, the promised seed) and fulfilled in Revelation.
p3: This unity across such diversity is a real pointer to a single divine Author behind the human authors — a striking datum honestly held as a pointer, not claimed as proof all by itself.
evidence: Sixty-six books, roughly forty authors — kings, shepherds, fishermen, a physician, a Rabbinically trained scholar — writing in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek across some 1,500 years, yet telling one story from creation to new creation. Literary critic Northrop Frye, no conservative Christian, devoted The Great Code (1982) to the Bible’s imaginative unity and its shaping influence on the whole of Western literature, tracing a system of typology no editorial committee could have designed.
practice_q: Without looking: how many books, authors, years, and languages span the Bible — and name two typological threads that begin in Genesis or Exodus and are fulfilled in the New Testament.
convo: If you asked forty strangers across fifteen centuries to each write on God, guilt, and hope without coordinating, what would you expect — and what would you make of it if one coherent story came back?

title: The Reliability of the Gospel Eyewitnesses
tagline: The Gospels contain evidence of genuine eyewitness testimony
p1: The Gospels name specific minor characters — Bartimaeus, Simon of Cyrene and his sons Alexander and Rufus, Joseph of Arimathea — people the first readers could still ask about the events.
p2: That naming pattern, plus embarrassing details the writers had no reason to keep and precise Palestinian geography, matches eyewitness testimony rather than legendary development — the case Richard Bauckham makes in Jesus and the Eyewitnesses.
p3: So the Gospels are early, local testimony rooted in people who were there — serious historical evidence, not anonymous folklore. Good provenance earns the testimony a fair hearing; whether the most astonishing parts are true is then argued on its own evidence.
evidence: Mark names blind Bartimaeus (Mark 10:46) and Simon of Cyrene "the father of Alexander and Rufus" (Mark 15:21) — one-time characters named, Bauckham argues, because the community could verify events with them. Gospel names match first-century Palestinian frequencies documented from ossuaries and papyri. Incidental details — six water jars at Cana, 153 fish, green grass at the feeding — read like remembered testimony, and the accounts keep awkward material like Peter's denial and the women finding the tomb.
practice_q: Without looking: why does Mark bother to name Simon of Cyrene's sons Alexander and Rufus, and what does Bauckham argue the Gospels' naming pattern shows about their sources?
convo: Storytellers polish away the embarrassing bits — yet the Gospels keep Peter's denial and put women, whose testimony was undervalued then, at the empty tomb first. Why do you think those details survived?

title: The Dead Sea Scrolls and Old Testament Reliability
tagline: Ancient manuscripts confirm the Old Testament text has been preserved with extraordinary accuracy
p1: Before 1947, our oldest Hebrew Old Testament manuscripts dated to around 900-1000 AD — leaving a thousand-year gap where sceptics could argue the text had drifted.
p2: The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered at Qumran from 1947, contain manuscripts from roughly 250-100 BC — and the Great Isaiah Scroll, all 66 chapters, is virtually identical to the Masoretic text behind modern translations.
p3: So the Old Testament was preserved with extraordinary fidelity — and texts like Isaiah 53 demonstrably predate Jesus. That vindicates the copying, not automatically the message: whether the content is true is argued on separate grounds, and the Scrolls clear the ground for that conversation.
evidence: Discovered from 1947 at Qumran, roughly 900 manuscripts cover every Old Testament book except Esther, dated c. 250 BC to 68 AD. The Great Isaiah Scroll (c. 125-100 BC) contains all 66 chapters and is virtually identical to the Masoretic text — in the famous Isaiah 53 chapter only 17 letters differ, mostly spelling, across 1,000 years of copying. Millar Burrows, who led the publication project, marvelled at how little the scroll differed from the text we already had.
practice_q: Without looking: what was our oldest Hebrew manuscript before 1947, how old is the Great Isaiah Scroll, how closely did the two texts match — and what does that prove versus what it doesn't?
convo: A complete Isaiah scroll copied a century before Jesus turned out to be virtually identical to the text in a modern Bible. Does that change how you picture a thousand years of hand-copying?

title: The Early Date of the New Testament
tagline: The Gospels were written within the lifetime of eyewitnesses
p1: Paul's letters — our earliest Christian documents — date to roughly 48-65 AD, and the creed Paul quotes in 1 Corinthians 15 goes back to within about 2-5 years of the crucifixion.
p2: Acts ends abruptly around 62 AD without mentioning Paul's death, Nero's persecution, or Jerusalem's fall in 70 AD — a silence (an inference, honestly held) most naturally explained by early composition; on any mainstream dating the Gospels fall within eyewitnesses' lifetimes.
p3: Early does not automatically mean true — but documents circulating while eyewitnesses lived were open to correction, which removes the objection that legend had long, undetected years to grow.
evidence: Paul's letters date to c. 48-65 AD, and the 1 Corinthians 15 creed — which Paul says he "received" — goes back to within about 2-5 years of the crucifixion. Acts ends around 62 AD with Paul awaiting trial, never mentioning his death, the Neronian persecution, or Jerusalem's fall; the Gospels record Jesus's prediction of the temple's destruction (Mark 13:2) yet never note its fulfilment — silences most naturally explained by pre-70 composition.
practice_q: Without looking: which events does Acts conspicuously never mention, what does that suggest about when Luke-Acts was written — and why is it important to concede this is a strong inference rather than a knockout proof?
convo: Acts follows Paul for chapters and then just stops — mid-story, before his trial's verdict, before Jerusalem fell. What do you think is the simplest explanation for a book ending like that?

title: The Argument from the Jewishness of Jesus
tagline: The Gospels accurately portray Jesus within his first-century Jewish context
p1: The Jesus of the Gospels is thoroughly Jewish — parables, rabbinic-style Torah debates with Pharisees, festival observance, preserved Aramaic words like Talitha koum, Abba, and Eloi Eloi lama sabachthani.
p2: By the second century Christianity was predominantly Gentile and increasingly estranged from its Jewish roots; later inventors would have produced a Hellenised Jesus — not one who fits, casually and precisely, a Jewish world that vanished when the Temple fell in 70 AD.
p3: So the Gospel traditions come from authentic first-century Palestinian Jewish sources. A true setting does not by itself prove every event — but it dismantles the picture of the Gospels as late, foreign legend, and it is held with gratitude toward Judaism, never against it.
evidence: The Gospels preserve Jesus's Aramaic words — Talitha koum, Abba, Eloi Eloi lama sabachthani — his parables and Torah debates, and a working Temple world that vanished in 70 AD. E.P. Sanders's rigorous historical scholarship places Jesus thoroughly within first-century Judaism, and the Jewish scholar Géza Vermes, who did not believe in Jesus's divinity, insisted he can only be understood as a first-century Jew of Galilee. Second-century Gentile Christians would not have invented this texture.
practice_q: Without looking: name two Aramaic phrases of Jesus the Greek Gospels preserve, and explain why a thoroughly Jewish Jesus is evidence against second-century Gentile invention.
convo: Some of the strongest voices recovering the Jewish Jesus have been Jewish and secular historians, not apologists. What do you make of sceptical scholarship agreeing the Gospels fit first-century Galilee so exactly?

title: The Argument from Undesigned Coincidences
tagline: Interlocking incidental details across independent accounts — the fingerprint of real memory, not coordinated invention
p1: Sometimes one Gospel raises a small puzzle another Gospel, on a different subject, accidentally solves: Jesus asks Philip, of all people, where to buy bread (John 6:5) — Luke 9:10 places the feeding near Bethsaida, and John 1:44 notes Philip was from Bethsaida.
p2: The pattern recurs: Luke's blindfold (22:64) explains Matthew's taunt "Prophesy — who hit you?" (26:67-68); Joanna, wife of Chuza, Herod's household manager (Luke 8:3), explains how the movement knew palace talk; Mark's "green grass" (6:39) fits John's Passover dating (6:4).
p3: Forgers do not plant puzzles in their own work and bury the solutions in someone else's; this unforced interlocking is how independent testimony to real events behaves — a cumulative pointer to genuine memory, not by itself a proof of any miracle.
evidence: John 6:5 has Jesus ask Philip where to buy bread — unexplained, until Luke 9:10 places the feeding near Bethsaida and John 1:44 notes Philip was from Bethsaida. Luke's blindfold (22:64) explains Matthew's taunt (26:67-68); Joanna, wife of Herod's household manager (Luke 8:3), explains palace knowledge. William Paley (Horae Paulinae, 1790) and J.J. Blunt (1847) pioneered the method; Lydia McGrew's Hidden in Plain View (2017) revived it for this generation.
practice_q: Without looking: reconstruct the Philip-and-the-bread coincidence — which three texts interlock, and why does copying between authors fail to explain a question in one document answered incidentally in another?
convo: When two friends describe the same afternoon, their small offhand details quietly fit together. Have you ever noticed the Gospels doing exactly that — one book accidentally answering a puzzle another leaves open?

title: The Argument from Palestinian Names
tagline: The Gospels get first-century Jewish name-statistics right — a test forgers could not have known existed
p1: Names are an accidental time stamp: Tal Ilan's lexicon of thousands of ancient Jewish names shows which were common in Palestine — rankings that differ sharply from Jewish communities abroad.
p2: Richard Bauckham ran the test: the Gospels and Acts match the Palestinian rankings with remarkable precision — Simon and Joseph top both lists — and they disambiguate common names (Simon Peter, Simon the Zealot, Mary Magdalene) while leaving rare names like Philip and Thomas bare.
p3: No ancient forger knew this test existed, and the late apocryphal gospels flunk it — so the traditions were formed early and locally in first-century Palestine. That is a pointer to provenance, one stubborn thread in the cable, not by itself proof of every event.
evidence: Tal Ilan's lexicon catalogues thousands of named Palestinian Jews (c. 330 BC to AD 200) from tomb inscriptions, documents, and Josephus. Bauckham found the Gospels and Acts match those rankings: Simon and Joseph top both lists, Mary and Salome lead the women, in roughly the right proportions. Common names carry qualifiers — Simon Peter, Simon the Zealot, Mary Magdalene — while rare names stand alone. Diaspora communities favoured different names, and late apocryphal gospels break the pattern.
practice_q: Without looking: which two male names top both Tal Ilan's Palestinian database and the Gospels, and why does the disambiguation habit — tagging Simons and Marys but not Philips — point to real community memory?
convo: You can roughly date a story set in 1950s Liverpool by counting its Johns and Margarets. Did you know the Gospels pass exactly that kind of name-statistics test for first-century Palestine — one no ancient writer knew was measurable?

title: The Argument from the Big Bang
tagline: Modern cosmology gives strong evidence that the universe had a beginning.
p1: Whatever begins to exist has a cause (note the careful wording — not 'everything has a cause').
p2: Strong evidence — the expansion of the universe and the 2003 Borde-Guth-Vilenkin theorem — indicates the universe began to exist, though some models still contest an absolute beginning.
p3: Therefore the universe has a cause — one that, having made space, time, and matter, must itself be spaceless, timeless, immaterial, enormously powerful, and (Craig argues) personal.
evidence: Atheist cosmologist Alexander Vilenkin: 'All the evidence we have says the universe had a beginning.' The BGV theorem (Physical Review Letters, 2003) shows any on-average expanding universe cannot be past-eternal. Nobel laureate Arno Penzias, co-discoverer of the cosmic microwave background: 'The best data we have are exactly what I would have predicted, had I nothing to go on but the five Books of Moses, the Psalms, and the Bible as a whole.'
practice_q: Without looking: what does the Borde-Guth-Vilenkin theorem show, and why does the argument say 'begins to exist' rather than 'everything has a cause'?
convo: Did you know many scientists expected an eternal universe and were startled when the evidence pointed to a beginning? What do you make of the universe having a start?

title: The Origin of Life Argument
tagline: The information content of DNA points beyond unguided chemistry.
p1: The first living cell requires DNA — a molecule storing not just complexity but specified, functional information.
p2: Evolution explains how life changes once it exists, but not where the first cell's information came from; after 70+ years, origin-of-life research still has no agreed account of it.
p3: Since the one cause we reliably know to produce specified information is a mind, an intelligent source is a reasonable — though contested and non-consensus — inference; many good scientists still expect a natural pathway.
evidence: Francis Crick, co-discoverer of DNA's structure and an atheist: the origin of life 'appears almost a miracle.' Stephen Meyer (Signature in the Cell, 2009) distinguishes specified information from mere complexity. Douglas Axe's Journal of Molecular Biology work (2004) estimated roughly 1 in 10^77 sequences of the right length fold into a functional protein — and a cell needs thousands working together.
practice_q: Without looking: what is the difference between complexity and specified information, and why is a design inference here a contested step rather than proven science?
convo: Evolution explains how life diversifies once it exists — but what do you think best explains how the very first living cell got started?

title: The Argument from Mathematics
tagline: The unreasonable effectiveness of mathematics points to a rational Creator.
p1: Abstract mathematics, invented by pure mathematicians with no physical application in mind, later describes physical reality with astonishing precision — sometimes predicting discoveries in advance.
p2: This 'unreasonable effectiveness' is mysterious and unexplained on a purely naturalistic worldview.
p3: On theism it is expected — a rational God created a rationally ordered universe and rational minds to understand it; this is an inference to the best explanation, not a knockdown proof.
evidence: Nobel laureate Eugene Wigner's 1960 paper called the fit 'bordering on the mysterious' — 'a wonderful gift which we neither understand nor deserve.' Complex numbers, invented as a curiosity, became essential to quantum mechanics; Riemann's non-Euclidean geometry became the geometry of spacetime. In 1928 Paul Dirac's equation predicted antimatter; Carl Anderson found the positron in 1932.
practice_q: Without looking: what did Wigner mean by the 'unreasonable effectiveness of mathematics,' and how does Dirac's prediction of antimatter illustrate it?
convo: Why do you think abstract mathematics, dreamed up on paper with no use in mind, ends up describing the physical universe so precisely?

title: The Cambrian Explosion
tagline: The sudden appearance of complex animal life is a real, unfinished puzzle.
p1: The fossil record shows most major animal body plans appearing in a geologically brief window (~5-10 million years) about 530 million years ago.
p2: New body plans require vast new genetic information; whether unguided Darwinian mechanisms suffice is genuinely debated — most working biologists say yes, but mainstream paleontologists acknowledge the puzzle is real.
p3: One contested, minority interpretation (Stephen Meyer) infers an input of new information from a mind — this is a non-consensus reading, not settled science, and most biologists treat the Cambrian as an open problem within evolution.
evidence: Darwin wrote in On the Origin of Species that the difficulty of the missing pre-Silurian fossils 'is very great,' hoping future finds would fill the gap; over 160 years of fossil hunting has confirmed the pattern rather than resolving it. Paleontologists James Valentine and Douglas Erwin have described the Cambrian explosion as straining conventional evolutionary explanation; Cambridge paleontologist Simon Conway Morris calls it a longstanding puzzle.
practice_q: Without looking: why did Darwin call the Cambrian explosion a difficulty for his theory, and why is the design inference a contested step rather than settled science?
convo: Darwin himself was troubled by how suddenly the major animal body plans appear in the rock — how do you think that's best explained?

title: The Argument from Cosmic Purposiveness
tagline: The universe appears structured, at many levels, toward intelligent life.
p1: Beyond fine-tuned constants, the universe shows converging conditions across physics, chemistry, and astronomy that all favour the emergence of conscious life.
p2: This convergence of independent conditions is highly improbable by chance and, on atheism, an unexplained brute fact.
p3: A purposive Creator is a reasonable — though contested and non-consensus — reading of the evidence; many careful thinkers read the same data without God, and this is offered as a pointer, not a proof.
evidence: Roger Penrose calculated the extraordinarily low-entropy order of the universe's beginning (he treats it as a puzzle for physics, not evidence for God). Simon Conway Morris documents convergent evolution — eyes arising independently over 40 times. Peter Ward and Donald Brownlee (Rare Earth, 2000), no friends of design arguments, catalogue how specific Earth's conditions are. Physicist-priest John Polkinghorne calls this directionality 'anthropic fruitfulness.'
practice_q: Without looking: name two of the converging conditions this argument appeals to, and explain why the design conclusion is an inference rather than a proof.
convo: When you notice how many separate conditions had to line up for conscious life to exist, does it feel more like accident or like intention to you?

title: The Privileged Planet Argument
tagline: The conditions that make Earth habitable also make it good for discovery.
p1: The same rare conditions that make Earth a good home — clear atmosphere, stable star, large moon, quiet galactic location — also make it unusually suited for scientific observation.
p2: This coincidence of habitability and discoverability is astronomically improbable by chance and is not accounted for by the anthropic principle, which only explains why we live somewhere survivable.
p3: On this minority, contested inference the convergence points to design; serious scientists push back that the pattern may be read into the data, so it is offered as one clue, not settled science.
evidence: Astronomer Guillermo Gonzalez and philosopher Jay Richards (The Privileged Planet, 2004) note the Moon is about 400 times smaller than the Sun and about 400 times closer, producing perfect total eclipses. Those brief eclipses let scientists first identify helium (1868) and confirm Einstein's light-bending prediction (Eddington, 1919) — discoveries that serve knowing, not survival.
practice_q: Without looking: how does the total solar eclipse illustrate the privileged-planet idea, and why is this treated as a contested minority claim?
convo: Isn't it curious that the very conditions letting us live here also let us study the whole universe — what do you make of that?

title: The Argument from the Laws of Nature
tagline: Rational, discoverable laws are best explained by a rational Lawgiver.
p1: The universe operates by precise, rational, mathematical laws that are the same everywhere and always.
p2: These laws are not physical — they are abstract rational structures matter obeys; on atheism it is mysterious why blind matter should obey them, and the order is left as an unexplained brute fact.
p3: On theism it is expected — a rational God imposed rational order on creation and made rational minds able to discover it; this is an inference to the best explanation, not a knockdown proof.
evidence: Nobel physicist Richard Feynman, an atheist: 'The fact that there are rules at all is a kind of miracle.' Einstein: 'The eternal mystery of the world is its comprehensibility.' Isaac Newton held this 'beautiful system' could 'only proceed from the counsel and dominion of an intelligent and powerful Being'; science as 'thinking God's thoughts after him' is a sentiment traditionally attributed to Kepler.
practice_q: Without looking: why is it puzzling on atheism that matter obeys rational mathematical laws, and what did Feynman concede about it?
convo: Why do you think the universe bothers to obey rational, mathematical rules at all, rather than simply being chaos?

title: The Shema and Trinitarian Monotheism
tagline: Deuteronomy 6:4 doesn't disprove the Trinity — it defines what the Trinity affirms
p1: The Shema declares there is exactly one God — and Christians affirm it without reservation; every historic Christian confession, including the Nicene Creed, begins with monotheism.
p2: The Shema answers how many Gods there are, not the internal nature of the one God — it shuts the door on rival gods while leaving the question of God's own inner life open.
p3: In 1 Corinthians 8:6 Paul — a trained Pharisee who had prayed the Shema since childhood — places Jesus inside the Shema's own words: 'one God, the Father… and one Lord, Jesus Christ,' confessing the one God as Father and Son.
evidence: Echad, the Hebrew 'one,' appears over 960 times and regularly spans compound unity; yachid, the word for an absolute singularity, is almost never used of God. The sturdier point is Paul: in 1 Corinthians 8:6, within roughly 25 years of the crucifixion, he distributes the Shema's two divine titles between the Father and Jesus — Richard Bauckham's God Crucified shows the New Testament identifies Jesus within the unique divine identity of YHWH without abandoning Jewish monotheism.
practice_q: Without looking: what does Paul do with the Shema's two divine titles — 'God' and 'Lord' — in 1 Corinthians 8:6, and why does it matter that he was a devout Jewish monotheist arguing against idolatry at the time?
convo: When the Shema says 'the LORD our God, the LORD is one,' do you think it's answering how many Gods there are — or describing the inner life of that one God?

title: The Trinity in the New Testament
tagline: Father, Son, and Spirit appear together repeatedly — in baptism, blessing, and mission
p1: Matthew 28:19 commands baptism 'in the name' — singular, not names — 'of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit': one name shared by three, the doctrine in seed form from Jesus himself.
