p2: At Jesus's baptism all three persons act at once — the Son in the water, the Spirit descending as a dove, the Father speaking from heaven — distinct, related, and unified in one divine event.
p3: The pattern runs through the earliest letters — the benediction of 2 Corinthians 13:14, the Trinitarian shape of Ephesians 1:3–14 — while the New Testament never loosens its grip on 'there is no God but one' (1 Corinthians 8:4).
evidence: Matthew 28:19 places Father, Son, and Spirit under one singular 'name' — and the Didache (c. 90–100 AD) already quotes it as the baptismal formula, within about two generations of the crucifixion. At the Jordan all three act simultaneously: the Son baptised, the Spirit descending, the Father speaking. Jesus promises 'allon Parakleton' — another Helper of the same kind — and the Spirit speaks, teaches, grieves, and can be lied to (Acts 5:3–4).
practice_q: Without looking: why is it significant that Matthew 28:19 says baptise in the NAME — singular — of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, and which early document quotes it as the standard baptismal formula?
convo: Have you ever noticed how often Father, Son, and Spirit simply appear together in the New Testament — at the baptism, in the blessings, in the Great Commission? What do you make of that pattern?

title: The Old Testament Foundations
tagline: The 'Let Us' passages, the Angel of the Lord, and Wisdom in Proverbs 8
p1: The Old Testament never spells out the Trinity — ancient Israel was fiercely monotheistic, and these passages are hints and preparations, not proofs; the doctrine is fully revealed only in the New Testament.
p2: The Angel of the Lord speaks as God, forgives and promises as God, and is identified with YHWH — yet is simultaneously sent by and distinguished from God: distinct from God, identified with God.
p3: Taken together — the 'Let Us' plurals of Genesis, personified Wisdom beside the Maker in Proverbs 8, the personal Spirit who can be grieved (Isaiah 63:10) — a consistent pattern of plurality within the one God emerges that the New Testament identifies as Father, Son, and Spirit.
evidence: The Angel of the Lord speaks from the burning bush as 'the God of Abraham' (Exodus 3); Hagar names him 'the God who sees me' (Genesis 16:13); Manoah cries 'We have seen God!' (Judges 13:22). Isaiah 48:16 distinguishes the Sovereign Lord, the one sent, and his Spirit in a single verse. None of these proves the Trinity by itself — they are hints the New Testament later recognises in the light of Christ.
practice_q: Without looking: name two things the Angel of the Lord does or receives that Scripture reserves for God alone — and state honestly what these Old Testament hints do and do not prove.
convo: In Exodus 3, the text says the Angel of the Lord appeared in the burning bush — and then that God called from it. Who do you think that figure is?

title: The Trinity in the Early Church
tagline: The doctrine was not invented at Nicaea — it was confessed from the very beginning
p1: Christians were worshipping Jesus as God long before any council: Ignatius of Antioch calls Jesus 'our God' around 107 AD, and the Aramaic-speaking church already prayed 'Marana tha' — 'Our Lord, come!'
p2: Tertullian coined the word 'Trinity' (Trinitas) and the formula 'one substance, three persons' around 200 AD — 125 years before Nicaea.
p3: Arius was the innovator — 'there was when he was not,' the Son as God's first creation — and Nicaea defended the received faith against him; only 2 of roughly 300 bishops refused to sign.
evidence: Around 112 AD the hostile Roman governor Pliny reported Christians gathering before dawn to sing 'to Christ, as to a god.' Ignatius of Antioch called Jesus 'our God' around 107 AD, on his way to martyrdom. Tertullian coined 'Trinity' and 'one substance, three persons' about 125 years before Nicaea. Arius — 'there was when he was not' — was the innovator; only 2 of roughly 300 bishops at Nicaea refused to sign the condemnation of Arianism.
practice_q: Without looking: who called Jesus 'our God' around 107 AD, what did Tertullian contribute roughly 125 years before Nicaea, and how many of the ~300 bishops at Nicaea refused to sign?
convo: You've probably heard that the Trinity was invented at Nicaea in 325. Would it surprise you that a pagan Roman governor reported Christians singing hymns 'to Christ, as to a god' two centuries earlier?

title: The Philosophical Defence of the Trinity
tagline: Is the Trinity a contradiction? Why 'one nature, three persons' is coherent
p1: A genuine contradiction affirms and denies the same thing in the same sense — 'one God and three Gods,' or 'one person and three persons,' would be contradictions; the Trinity claims neither.
p2: The doctrine says one nature — what God is — and three persons — who God is. Two different questions, two different answers; the numbers never answer the same question in the same sense.
p3: Both major models — the Social Trinity (Swinburne, and Moreland & Craig's 'Trinity monotheism') and the Latin Trinity (Augustine, Aquinas) — argue the doctrine is logically coherent; whether the social model preserves monotheism or drifts toward tritheism is a live debate on both sides, and neither model claims to fully comprehend the divine nature.
evidence: A contradiction requires P and not-P in the same sense; 'one nature, three persons' answers two different questions. Both the Social model (Swinburne; Moreland & Craig) and the Latin model (Augustine, Aquinas) defend the doctrine's coherence, united by perichoresis — the mutual indwelling Jesus voices in John 14:10–11 and 17:21. Honesty matters here too: the popular analogies — water, the egg, three roles — break down into modalism, partialism, or tritheism when pressed.
practice_q: Without looking: state precisely why 'one nature, three persons' is not the same claim as 'one God and three Gods' — and name the two main philosophical models of the Trinity with one representative of each.
convo: When people say the Trinity is bad math — that one can't equal three — what do you think the doctrine is actually claiming? Would it help to look at what it has never claimed?

title: Responding to Muslims on the Trinity
tagline: Tawhid, shirk, and why the Trinity is not polytheism
p1: Begin by agreeing: there is one God. Christians affirm tawhid — one God, one divine being — and reject what the Qur'an rejects: polytheism, a divine wife, a physical child, and any trinity of God, Jesus, and Mary (Surah 5:116). No Christian creed has ever taught those things.
p2: The Qur'an itself calls Jesus the Word of God (Kalimatullah) and a Spirit from him (Surah 4:171) — which raises the gentle question: is God's Word eternal? If yes, there has always existed an eternal, personal, divine Word — which is what Christians mean by the Son.
p3: 'God is love' (1 John 4:8) — and love requires a beloved. In the Trinity, God has always been love within his own life, Father loving Son in the Spirit; it is hard to see how a strictly solitary God could have been essentially loving before creation.
evidence: Surah 4:171 calls Jesus God's Word (Kalimatullah) and a Spirit from him; Surah 5:116 rejects a trinity of God, Jesus, and Mary — which Christians reject as firmly as Muslims do, along with any physical son. The Qur'an names Allah Al-Wadud, the Loving (Surah 11:90, 85:14) — yet love requires an eternal object. And the Asharite–Mutazilite debates over the divine attributes show the relation between divine unity and distinction is a live question within Islamic theology itself.
practice_q: Without looking: what does Surah 4:171 call Jesus, what does Surah 5:116 actually attack, and what question does the Christian gently raise about God's Word?
convo: Can I say something you might not expect? I agree with you that there is only one God. Could I ask what you've been told Christians mean by the Trinity — so we can talk about the real doctrine rather than a caricature?

title: Responding to Jehovah's Witnesses
tagline: John 1:1, the New World Translation, and why Arianism was rejected by the early church
p1: Jehovah's Witnesses sincerely teach that only the Father is God in the full sense and that the Son is God's first and greatest creation — so the real question between us is sharp and simple: is Jesus a creature, or the Creator?
p2: John 1:1 holds both halves together: the Word was WITH God (distinct persons) and the Word WAS God (one divine nature). The Greek anarthrous predicate construction is qualitative — the Word has the very nature of God — and virtually every Greek scholar rejects 'a god.'
p3: Jesus consistently accepts the worship that every faithful angel and apostle in the New Testament refuses (Revelation 22:8–9; Acts 10:25–26) — and receives Thomas's 'My Lord and my God!' (John 20:28) with a blessing, not a correction.
evidence: Julius Mantey, whose Greek grammar the Watchtower cited, publicly protested the misuse of his work and demanded a retraction; Bruce Metzger judged 'a god' unjustified by the grammatical evidence. John 1:3 adds that all things were made through the Word — a created 'a god' cannot be the agent of all creation. And Ignatius of Antioch calls Jesus 'our God' around 107 AD, showing Arius, not the church, was the innovator.
practice_q: Without looking: what does the anarthrous predicate construction in John 1:1c actually indicate in Greek — and what happens every time someone other than Jesus receives worship (proskuneo) in the New Testament?
convo: Why do you think Jesus accepts Thomas calling him 'my Lord and my God' — when in Revelation an angel immediately stops John's worship and says 'Worship God'?

title: Responding to Mormons on the Trinity
tagline: The King Follett discourse, separate Gods, and why the Bible doesn't teach LDS theology
p1: State LDS teaching accurately and kindly, in its own words: Father, Son, and Spirit are three separate beings one in purpose; the Father has a body of flesh and bones (D&C 130:22); and in the King Follett discourse (1844) Joseph Smith taught that God was once a man who progressed to godhood.
p2: Isaiah 43–46 is God's own answer: 'Before me no god was formed, nor will there be one after me' (43:10) — ruling out any god before YHWH, any god after him, and therefore eternal progression.
p3: The Book of Mormon itself (Alma 11:28–29, 44; 2 Nephi 31:21) confesses 'one Eternal God' — offered not as a gotcha but as an open door for gracious conversation with a sincere friend.
evidence: Isaiah is emphatic: 'Before me no god was formed, nor will there be one after me' (43:10); 'Is there any God besides me?… I know not one' (44:8). Scripture adds that God is spirit (John 4:24), unchanging (Malachi 3:6), 'from everlasting to everlasting' (Psalm 90:2), and 'not a man' (Numbers 23:19). The Book of Mormon itself calls Father, Son, and Holy Ghost 'one Eternal God' (Alma 11:44; 2 Nephi 31:21) — closer to orthodoxy than the later Doctrine and Covenants.
practice_q: Without looking: what does Isaiah 43:10 say about gods formed before or after YHWH — and why does that directly challenge the LDS doctrine of eternal progression?
convo: The Book of Mormon itself calls the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost 'one Eternal God' in Alma 11 — could we read that passage together alongside Isaiah 43–46 and see where it leads?

title: Responding to Modalism and Oneness Pentecostalism
tagline: Jesus Only theology, the modes of God, and why the baptism narrative refutes modalism
p1: Oneness theology holds that God is one single person who appears in three roles or modes — Father, then Son, then Spirit. The instinct to guard God's oneness is honoured; the mistake is collapsing three real persons into one.
p2: At Jesus's baptism the Son is in the water, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Father speaks about the Son — three at the same moment. Masks take turns; persons can share a moment. One person cannot simultaneously speak, be spoken to, and descend upon himself.
p3: Jesus prays to the Father — 'not as I will, but as you will' — counts himself and the Father as two witnesses under Moses's law (John 8:17–18), and says the Father loved him 'before the foundation of the world' (John 17:24). Real relations require really distinct persons; you cannot love a mask.
evidence: Modalism was condemned in the third century: Tertullian wrote Against Praxeas — who, he charged, had 'crucified the Father' — and Hippolytus and Origen wrote against Sabellius. At the Jordan the Father speaks about the Son while the Spirit descends on him; in John 8:17–18 Jesus counts the Father and himself as two witnesses; and the Didache (c. 90–100 AD) shows the threefold formula of Matthew 28:19, not 'Jesus only,' was the early church's baptismal practice.
practice_q: Without looking: how does the baptism of Jesus refute modalism — and how do you answer the claim that Acts 2:38 ('in the name of Jesus') contradicts Matthew 28:19?
convo: I love how seriously you take the oneness of God — so do I. Can I ask what you make of Jesus saying the Father loved him before the foundation of the world? Can a mode be loved before it is ever worn?

title: The Eternal Generation of the Son
tagline: What 'begotten, not made' means — the Nicene doctrine and its biblical foundations
p1: 'Begotten, not made': the Son is not a creature brought into being out of nothing but is of the Father's own being — God from God, Light from Light, true God from true God. Begotten is the opposite of created, not a synonym for it.
p2: Eternal generation is a timeless, necessary relation, not a past event — there was never a moment, never even a 'before,' when the Father existed without the Son. If the Father is always Father, the Son is always Son.
p3: The doctrine preserves three truths at once: the Son's full divinity (homoousios with the Father), his genuine distinction from the Father (against modalism), and God's immutability (against Arianism — God was never without his Word).
evidence: John 5:26 — the Father 'has granted the Son also to have life in himself' — self-existence eternally received, the essence of eternal generation. Hebrews 1:3 calls the Son 'the radiance of God's glory': the fathers' sun-and-light picture, enshrined in the creed as 'Light from Light' — though the analogy has limits — the sun's light is not a distinct person. Athanasius argued Arianism would mean God changed — once without his Word — contradicting divine immutability (Malachi 3:6).
practice_q: Without looking: what is the difference between being 'begotten' and being 'made' — and why did Athanasius argue that the Arian 'there was when he was not' would violate God's immutability?
convo: When the creed says Jesus is 'begotten, not made,' what do you think those words are guarding? Would it change anything for you if the one who came to save us were a creature rather than God himself?
